chunk_summary: Guidelines for preventing refeeding syndrome in high-risk patients, emphasizing thiamine supplementation, stepwise calorie advancement, and electrolyte monitoring in outpatient and inpatient settings.
learning_objectives: Understand the criteria for identifying high-risk refeeding syndrome patients.
Learn the protocol for thiamine supplementation and electrolyte correction before refeeding.
Implement stepwise calorie advancement and caregiver education for safe refeeding.
Monitor and manage refeeding risks in outpatient and inpatient care settings.
key_facts: Refeeding risk is high in cases of severe undernutrition, >5 days of very low intake, or rapid weight loss.
Baseline electrolytes (phosphate, potassium, magnesium), glucose, and hydration status must be checked and corrected before refeeding.
Thiamine should be administered before and during refeeding to prevent cardiac and neurologic complications.
Outpatient care requires a 'safe start' plan with daily caregiver monitoring and early follow-up within 24–72 hours.
In inpatient settings, frequent electrolyte monitoring is critical, especially during the first 72 hours.
Document refeeding risk level, thiamine plan, and electrolyte monitoring schedule for safety.
reference_quote: Give thiamine before and during refeeding for high-risk patients, following institutional dosing, because deficiency can precipitate cardiac and neurologic complications when carbohydrates are reintroduced. (Source: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/32115791/)
